page.title: How to Hire a CTO in the UK
page.intro:
The Chief Technology Officer role has fragmented significantly over the past decade, and the profile of the ideal CTO varies more dramatically by organisational context than almost any other C-suite position. The CTO of a 40-person SaaS startup — who should be deep in the code, setting technical architecture, and hiring the first ten engineers — is a fundamentally different role from the CTO of a 1,000-person enterprise technology division, where the responsibilities are primarily organisational, strategic, and vendor-relationship oriented. Getting this distinction right before beginning a search is the most important factor in CTO appointment quality.

The UK CTO market in 2026 is characterised by high demand, relatively tight supply of candidates who have built and scaled engineering organisations at the relevant scale, and compensation expectations that continue to be influenced by US technology company benchmarks — particularly for candidates who have worked for or been offered roles at major US tech businesses. The combination of these factors means that the most capable CTOs are approached very frequently, have multiple live conversations at any point, and can afford to be selective about the opportunities they consider seriously.

CTO assessment requires technical credibility on the part of the assessing consultant. A search firm that lacks genuine technology sector relationships and the technical vocabulary to engage CTO candidates substantively will find that the strongest candidates disengage quickly. Peer-to-peer credibility — the ability to have a technically literate conversation about engineering culture, architecture decisions, team building, and the specific technology challenges of the client organisation — is a prerequisite for effective CTO search at the most senior levels.
considerations: Define the CTO archetype clearly: "hands-on architect," "engineering leader," "technology strategist," or "digital transformation lead" — these require very different profiles and draw on different candidate pools.
Involve your most senior engineering leaders in the assessment: a CTO who cannot earn the respect of the VP Engineering and principal engineers will fail regardless of their strategic capability.

Assess technology judgment, not just leadership: ask candidates to work through a real architectural problem relevant to your stack, and have a technically literate assessor evaluate the quality of their thinking.

Be honest about your engineering organisation's current state: CTOs who take roles on the basis of a misleadingly positive picture of the team, codebase, or culture are consistently among those who fail fastest.
Consider fractional or interim CTO arrangements if the search will take more than 12 weeks: the productivity cost of a technology leadership gap is very high in engineering-intensive organisations.
page.salaryRange: £150,000–£400,000+ depending on organisation size, stage, and equity structure
page.searchFee: 25%–33% of agreed compensation; equity-adjusted packages sometimes used as the base
page.timeline: 10–14 weeks to accepted offer; notice periods of 3 months are typical in UK technology
page.whySearch: The best CTOs are embedded in technically interesting work, surrounded by engineers who respect them, and receiving regular unsolicited approaches that they largely ignore. Reaching them requires more than a LinkedIn message — it requires a search consultant who can have a technically credible conversation, a well-articulated account of the opportunity, and a relationship-based approach that distinguishes genuine executive search from the background noise of recruitment outreach. The technology talent market is uniquely competitive; executive search firms with genuine technology sector relationships consistently outperform general recruiters by a significant margin for CTO and VP Engineering appointments.
